problem_statement: Code review is central but tedious and expensive to carry out, and automated code review methods have had only modest success; the paper investigates whether LLMs can aid reviews by flagging security vulnerabilities and validating that code meets its intended functionality.
magnitude_evidence: Code reviews 'can be costly and tedious to carry out'; automated code review methods proposed 'so far, though, with modest success'; empirical studies suggest 'security vulnerabilities are rare in real codebases' (~5% dirty observations)
insufficiency_evidence: Only 36.7% of LLM-generated descriptions can be associated with true CWE vulnerabilities; best RQ1 F1 score only 37.9%; all open-source models 'perform on par with random or dummy classifiers'
solution_mechanisms: LLM software functional validation without execution
